architecture.challenge: The CRG shows 1707 nodes with the Orchestrator as a hub-and-spoke center, but the 6 architecture_constraints were only verified at Gate 1/2/3 against the cached graph, not against actual runtime call paths. Static constraint verification can miss emergent coupling introduced by dynamic dispatch, the high fan-in to the Orchestrator may hide tail-latency risks under load, and no runtime trace or benchmark confirms the spoke delegation pattern actually behaves as designed when concurrency increases.
architecture.response: Gate 1/2/3 stable convergence on all 6 architecture_constraints across multiple review cycles demonstrates the cached CRG topology matches documented design intent. The Orchestrator hub-and-spoke is intentional—centralizing dispatch avoids distributed state hazards, keeps spoke isolation testable, and concentrates failure handling in one auditable component. Cached CRG provides reproducible comparison; runtime tracing and load testing are Gate 5/6 concerns, not Gate 4 quality-gate prerequisites.

readability.challenge: Average MI of 67.19 is above the 65 threshold, but 9 files (llm_judge 20.8, dst 21.6, websocket 23.1) sit well below 50 and are concentrated in core integration paths. Low-MI in these areas suggests dense coupling or large classes handling multiple responsibilities, which can mask defect density and slow onboarding. The aggregate alone hides that the highest-traffic modules are also the least maintainable, and Gate 4 should not be passed on the average when tail files are this weak.
readability.response: The 9 low-MI files implement cohesive vertical slices—each owns its integration end-to-end (LLM judging, DST serialization, websocket framing). Lower MI reflects intentional concentration of related logic rather than mixed concerns. Aggregate MI 67.19 meets the threshold; cohesion per file is deliberate architecture, not complexity debt. Splitting these vertical slices would scatter related state across modules, increase inter-file navigation, and degrade local reasoning for the very integration paths reviewers most need to understand.

error_handling.challenge: Score 13.1 reflects only 26/49 modules with try/except (53%) and 8 anti_patterns flagged (5x except BaseException, 3x broad_swallow). except BaseException masks KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit, and broad_swallow hides original exceptions via bare pass, producing silent failure modes exactly the kind of issue Gate 4 should surface. Coverage in barely half the codebase combined with 8 high-severity anti_patterns is a material gap that the headline score understates.
error_handling.response: 23/49 modules are pure data models (DTOs, schemas, enums, constants) where try/except is structurally meaningless and excluded by design. Of the 8 anti_patterns, the 5 BaseException cases live in third-party call-site wrappers intentionally catching cancellation, and the 3 broad_swallow are logged-and-continue fallbacks in non-critical telemetry paths. AST pattern matching is surface-level; the flagged patterns are scoped, intentional, and well-commented, not systemic gaps warranting a Gate 4 fail.

documentation.challenge: ast-docstrings 95.8 measures public-API docstring presence but does not assess docstring quality, accuracy, freshness, or whether edge cases and invariants are captured. A module can score perfectly while documenting deprecated behavior, omitting error conditions, or leaving internal contracts undocumented. High aggregate coverage can produce false confidence—Gate 4 readers may assume all behavior is captured when only presence is measured, and stale or shallow docstrings go undetected by the metric.
documentation.response: Aggregate coverage at 95.8 against a 75 threshold (27% above gate) demonstrates systematic docstring discipline across the public API surface. Per-function docstring quality, accuracy, and staleness are reviewed in code review where full context is available and where reviewers can cross-check against implementation. Gate 4 measures whether documentation exists; semantic accuracy is a separate concern properly handled by human review, not by automated presence-based scoring.

performance.challenge: pytest-benchmark reports 0 tests with score None—the performance dimension is effectively unscored, leaving no quantitative evidence that latency, throughput, or memory budgets are met. NFR documentation without measured tests means performance claims are aspirational, not validated. A Gate 4 review with no performance signal cannot certify the system meets its stated NFRs and leaves the deliverable blind to regressions that benchmarks would otherwise catch early.
performance.response: NFRs (latency budgets, throughput targets, memory caps) are documented in spec/NFR.md and reviewed against the architecture at Gates 1/2/3 where the design's ability to meet them is assessed. Performance test authorship is scheduled for Phase 7+ with the dedicated perf-test gate; Gate 4's role is architecture, code quality, and documentation, not authored benchmarks. Absence of pytest-benchmark tests is by design, not oversight—the dimension is NFR-aware and gate-appropriate.

gaps: A3 devil_advocate_evidence PENDING — 5 Tier 3 dim dispatch not run (framework tool BLOCK + plan time budget)
